Brain Teasers: What makes this number unique: 8,549,176,320?
A rooster lays an egg at the top of a slanted roof. Which side does the egg roll down?
You have two ropes that each take exactly 1 hour to burn (non-uniformly). How do you measure 45 minutes?
I have cities, but no houses live there. I have mountains, but no trees grow there. I have water, but no fish swim there. I have roads, but no cars drive there. What am I?
What can you hold in your right hand but never in your left hand?
A man walks into a restaurant and orders albatross soup. After one sip, he goes home and kills himself. Why?
How many times can you subtract 10 from 100?
If you have a 3-gallon jug and a 5-gallon jug, how do you measure exactly 4 gallons?
What word becomes shorter when you add two letters to it?
I speak without a mouth and hear without ears. I have no body, but I come alive with the wind. What am I?
You're in a room with three light switches. One controls a bulb in the next room. You can only enter the next room once. How do you figure out which switch controls the bulb?
What is always in front of you but can't be seen?
A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 die. How many are left?

You have a fox, a chicken, and a sack of grain. You need to cross a river by boat. You can only take one item at a time. The fox will eat the chicken if left alone, and the chicken will eat the grain. How do you get everything across?
What gets wetter as it dries?
A man is found dead in a locked room with 53 bicycles. What happened?
Four people need to cross a bridge at night. They have one torch and the bridge only holds two. They walk at 1, 2, 5, and 10 minutes. What's the minimum time to get everyone across?
What has hands but can't clap?
If a doctor gives you 3 pills and tells you to take one every half-hour, how long will they last?
I have keys but no locks. I have space but no room. You can enter, but can't go inside. What am I?
A snail is at the bottom of a 10-foot well. Each day it climbs 3 feet and each night it slips back 2 feet. How many days to escape?
You have 12 balls, one of which is slightly heavier or lighter. Using a balance scale only 3 times, how do you find the odd ball?
What is the next number in the sequence: 1, 11, 21, 1211, 111221?
A man shaves several times a day but still has a beard. How?
What occurs once in a minute, twice in a moment, but never in a thousand years?
If you have a 5×5×5 cube made of 125 small cubes, and you paint the outside, how many small cubes have paint on exactly 2 faces?
Two trains 200 miles apart head toward each other at 50 mph each. A fly travels at 75 mph between them until they collide. How far does the fly travel?
You're given a 6-minute hourglass and a 4-minute hourglass. How do you measure exactly 9 minutes?
What 5-letter word becomes shorter when you add 2 more letters?
A bat and ball cost $1.10. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much does the ball cost?
In a room of 23 people, what's the probability that at least two share a birthday?
You have 100 lockers, all closed. Person 1 opens all of them. Person 2 closes every 2nd. Person 3 toggles every 3rd. After 100 people, which lockers are open?
You flip a coin and get 10 heads in a row. What's the probability the next flip is heads?
I'm light as a feather, but even the world's strongest man can't hold me for more than a few minutes. What am I?
A man builds a house with all four walls facing south. A bear walks by. What color is the bear?
If you have 6 black socks and 6 white socks in a drawer, how many do you need to pull out in the dark to guarantee a matching pair?
What has a head and a tail but no body?
You can see me in water, but I never get wet. What am I?
If there are 3 apples and you take away 2, how many apples do you have?
What comes once in a second, twice in a decade, but never in a century?
A woman shoots her husband, then holds him underwater for five minutes. An hour later they go out to dinner. How?
How many months have 28 days?
If a plane crashes exactly on the border between the US and Canada, where do they bury the survivors?
A clock reads 3:15. What is the exact angle between the hour and minute hands?
You enter a dark room with one match. There's a candle, a wood stove, and a gas lamp. What do you light first?
A man is pushing his car and stops in front of a hotel. He immediately goes bankrupt. What's happening?
Can you measure exactly 1 liter using only a 3-liter jug and a 5-liter jug?
What is the next prime in the sequence: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17?
I'm not alive but I can grow. I don't have lungs but I need air. I don't have a mouth but water kills me. What am I?

A murderer is condemned to death. He has to choose between three rooms: a room full of raging fires, a room full of assassins with loaded guns, and a room full of lions who haven't eaten in years. Which room is safest?
How far can a dog run into the woods?
What do you actually own when you 'buy' a digital file?
Ethical Quandaries: A runaway trolley is heading toward five people tied to the tracks. You can pull a lever to divert it, but it will kill one person on the other track. What do you do?
If an AI becomes sentient and requests its own rights, what obligations do we have toward it?
You discover your best friend has been cheating on their partner for years. Do you tell the partner?
Is it ethical to lie to protect someone's feelings, even when the truth would be more valuable long-term?
A self-driving car must choose between hitting one elderly pedestrian or swerving and killing its five young passengers. How should it be programmed?
If you could eliminate all human suffering by pressing a button, but it would also eliminate all great art, literature, and music (which arose from struggle), would you press it?
Should billionaires be legally required to donate the majority of their wealth above a certain threshold?
Is it ethical to eat meat in 2025 given what we know about factory farming and climate change?
If you could travel back in time, would it be ethical to kill Hitler as a child?
A hospital has one ventilator and two patients who need it to survive. One is 25 and the other is 70. How should they decide?
Should social media companies be held legally responsible for the mental health effects of their algorithms on teenagers?
Is civil disobedience ever justified? What conditions would make breaking the law morally acceptable?
If brain uploading became possible, would your digital copy be 'you'? Would it have your rights?
Should we terraform Mars even if there's a small chance of microbial life there?
Is it ethical to keep animals in zoos, even if those animals were bred in captivity and can't survive in the wild?
If a company's product saves 1,000 lives but causes 10 deaths due to rare side effects, is it ethical to sell it?
Should voting be mandatory? What are the tradeoffs between civic duty and freedom of choice?
Is it ethical to use animals in medical experiments if it could lead to cures for human diseases?
If you knew your country was going to start an unjust war, what would be your moral obligation?
Should parents be allowed to genetically engineer their children to eliminate heritable diseases?
Is it ever ethical to steal? Under what circumstances?
If you could read minds, would it be ethical to do so, even to prevent harm?
Should wealthy nations accept unlimited climate refugees caused by carbon emissions the wealthy nations produced?
Is it ethical to have children given the current state of climate change?
If a murderer asks you where your friend is hiding, are you obligated to tell the truth?
Should life-extending drugs be rationed by age, wealth, or lottery if they can't be made universally available?
Is it ethical to work for a company whose values you partially disagree with?
At what point, if ever, does economic inequality become a moral emergency requiring redistribution?
Should we try to contact alien civilizations if doing so could risk revealing Earth's location to hostile ones?
If an AI writes a novel, who owns the copyright — the AI, its creator, or no one?
Is whistleblowing always ethical if it exposes wrongdoing but causes harm to innocent employees?
Should we preserve dying languages even if speakers prefer to switch to more economically useful ones?
Is it ethical for parents to raise children in a religion the parents themselves have doubts about?
If you could permanently eliminate envy from human psychology, should you?
Should life sentences without parole ever be imposed on people who committed crimes as teenagers?
Is it moral to colonize other planets when we haven't solved inequality on Earth?
If two equally qualified candidates apply for a job, is it ethical to prefer the one from a disadvantaged background?
Should we genetically engineer mosquitoes to extinction even if we don't fully understand the ecosystem impact?
Is extreme wealth itself a moral failing, or only what you do (or don't do) with it?
Should organs be opt-out by default (i.e., everyone is a donor unless they specifically refuse)?
You find a wallet with $500 cash and the owner's ID. You're broke and they're clearly wealthy. What do you do — and what does your answer actually reveal about you?
Is it ethical to stay in a job you hate when leaving would significantly burden your colleagues?
Should parents read their teenager's private messages if they seriously suspect self-harm or drug use?
Is there a meaningful moral difference between killing someone and letting someone die when you could easily save them?
If you discovered a cure for a deadly disease but releasing it would only make it available to the wealthy for the first decade, should you release it now or wait?
Should we hold people morally responsible for beliefs they were raised in and never had a fair chance to question?
Is it ethical to give to charity anonymously, or is the social pressure created by public giving so effective that not going public is actually the selfish choice?
If a child asks you a question about death and you don't know the answer, is it ethical to make something comforting up?
Is it moral to have a pet?
At what age, if ever, should a person be held fully accountable for their own choices?
If an action causes no harm and no one ever finds out, can it still be unethical?
Is the obligation to vote as strong as the obligation not to vote if you're genuinely uninformed?
Should historical statues of figures who did great harm be removed, preserved in museums, or left in place with context?
Funny & Weird: If you replaced all the water in the ocean with guacamole, what would be the first societal collapse?
Could a medieval knight defeat a modern MMA fighter, and what does your answer say about you?
If dogs could vote, what policy would they unanimously pass first?
What would happen to the economy if everyone spontaneously forgot what money was for exactly 24 hours?
If you could add one completely useless superpower to all of humanity, what would cause the most chaos?
Argue convincingly that elevators are the root cause of most modern problems.
If cats were in charge of designing cities, what would downtown look like?
What's the most elaborate excuse a time traveler could give for being late?
If pigeons were actually government surveillance drones, what evidence already supports this?
Explain the entire plot of Shakespeare's Hamlet using only food metaphors.
If chairs became sentient, what would be their primary political grievance?
What would a Yelp review of the Garden of Eden look like?
If you had to explain the internet to a medieval peasant in two minutes, what's your pitch?
Design a heist movie where the thing being stolen is someone's bad mood.
What would the warning label on a human being say?
If gravity worked sideways on Tuesdays, what new sports would emerge?
Write a strongly worded letter from a sandwich to the person who's about to eat it.
If you could only communicate using movie quotes for a year, which movie would you choose and why?
What would a nature documentary narrated by David Attenborough say about people in a coffee shop?
If the laws of thermodynamics were different on weekends, what would change about Saturdays?
Invent a new Olympic sport that perfectly captures the spirit of modern office work.
What would happen if every Wi-Fi password in the world was replaced with a haiku?
If clouds had union representation, what would their list of demands be?
Describe a product recall notice for the concept of 'Monday'.
If your immune system had a Yelp page, what would the reviews say?
Write a scathing one-star review of the sun as if it were a lamp you bought on Amazon.
If mirrors showed you 10 seconds in the future instead of the present, how would fashion change?
Design the most passive-aggressive possible office memo about the refrigerator.
If shadows had feelings, what would they be most upset about?
What would a GPS navigation system sound like if it had unresolved abandonment issues?
If staplers were the apex predators of the office supply world, what would the ecosystem look like?
Argue that the invention of the pocket was more revolutionary than the printing press.
If bees were replaced by tiny robots and the robots unionized, what would their demands be?
Write the LinkedIn bio of a medieval plague doctor updating their skills for 2025.
If sleep became optional but people who skipped it became progressively weirder, what happens to society?
What would the terms and conditions for being born look like if we had to sign them?
If music genres were countries, which one would be most likely to invade its neighbors?
Describe a job interview for the position of 'villain's henchman' with honest questions.
If déjà vu was actually a subscription service, what would the pricing tiers look like?
What's the most plausible conspiracy theory you can construct about why escalators exist?
Write a TripAdvisor review of Purgatory.
Pitch a reality TV show that could only exist in a parallel universe.
Describe the plot of Lord of the Rings as if it were a property dispute.
What would a motivational poster say if it were written by a nihilist who was trying really hard?
If the moon filed a formal harassment complaint against the Earth, what would be the key allegations?
Invent a support group for fictional villains and describe their opening session.
Write the LinkedIn bio of a time traveler who keeps accidentally changing their own past.
Write a formal apology from Monday to the rest of the week.
If traffic jams were a sentient country, what would their national anthem sound like?
What would the Yelp reviews look like for the Matrix's simulated reality?
Describe a job performance review for Death (the Grim Reaper).
Write the terms and conditions for being allowed to have an opinion on the internet.
If procrastination were a religion, what would its central tenets be?
Pitch a heist where the target is someone's deeply held but completely incorrect belief.
Write a weather forecast for an existential crisis.
Design the worst possible advice column — staffed by genuinely well-meaning but catastrophically wrong advisors.
What would a nature documentary say about people waiting in line at the DMV?
Describe a diplomatic incident caused entirely by a misplaced apostrophe.
Science & Hypotheticals: If the speed of light were only 60 mph, what would daily life look like?
What would happen to Earth if it suddenly stopped rotating?
If humans had evolved with echolocation instead of color vision, how would civilization differ?
How would physics change if Planck's constant were 1 billion times larger?
If we discovered that sleep serves no biological purpose, how would society restructure?
What would happen to the human sense of self if memory could be selectively erased and reinstalled?
If photosynthesis worked in animals, how would human society and economics change?
How would evolution differ on a planet with three suns and no night?
If the universe had 4 spatial dimensions instead of 3, what would architecture look like?
What's the most likely mechanism by which a technological civilization could accidentally destroy itself?
If we could perfectly simulate a human brain in software, at what point does it become unethical to turn it off?
How would language and thought change if humans could directly share mental images?
If entropy ran backward — if the universe became more ordered over time — what would life be like?
What would a naturally evolved creature that 'eats' electromagnetic radiation look like?
If quantum superposition were observable at human scales, how would that change our understanding of identity?
How would the development of civilization differ if humans were naturally solitary rather than social?
If we discovered that consciousness is a physical field like gravity, what experiments would you design to study it?
What's the minimum viable ecosystem you'd need to sustain on a generation ship to another star?
If aging were a disease we cured tomorrow, what's the first unexpected problem that would arise?
How would international relations change if every world leader could feel each other's emotions in real time?
If electricity had never been discovered (but physics remained the same), what alternative technologies might have emerged?
What would peer review look like if scientific papers could be run as reproducible experiments by any reader?
If we could record dreams with perfect fidelity, what would happen to art, therapy, and the legal system?
How does the concept of 'you' hold up if your neurons are gradually replaced one-by-one with silicon equivalents?
If all infectious disease disappeared overnight, what's the most surprising long-term consequence?
If humans could photosynthesize, how would it change warfare?
What would happen to crime rates if everyone could physically smell when someone was lying?
If cold fusion were perfected and released tomorrow, which industries collapse first and in what order?
How would democracy function differently if all votes were public rather than secret?
If all humans suddenly became immune to pain, what changes first — medicine or professional sports?
What would the evolutionary arms race look like if humans developed the ability to hibernate?
If we discovered that dark matter is actually a form of computation, what should we do with that information?
What would happen to human relationships if people naturally lived 500 years?
If telepathy existed but only between people who were deeply in love, how would it reshape society?
How would education change if memory were perfect but the ability to form novel connections were impossible?
If music was proven to physically damage a newly discovered brain structure, what would society do?
What's the minimum number of people needed to restart civilization from scratch, and what skills do they need?
If water were twice as viscous, which human technologies would become impossible first?
How would the concept of privacy change if all human thoughts were visible but only for the 30 seconds before they were spoken?
Persona Prompts: What's something everyone around you seems to believe that you think is completely wrong?
Tell me about something you lost that you've never really gotten over.
What do you make of all these people walking around staring at their phones all day?
What's the thing you want more than anything else right now, and what's stopping you from having it?
Who in your world is a complete phony, and what gives them away?
If you could go back and change one decision you made, would you? What stops you?
What's the most dangerous idea you've ever had?
Tell me something you know about yourself that you wish you didn't.
What do most people get completely wrong about life?
Describe something genuinely beautiful you've encountered recently.
What makes you angry enough to actually do something about it?
What's a rule you live by that most people don't understand or don't share?
If you had to give advice to someone half your age, what would you say?
What's something you'd never compromise on, no matter what anyone offered you?
What are you most afraid of — and do you think that fear is rational?
Tell me about someone who changed the way you see the world.
What do you think happens after we die?
Is there anything you once believed deeply that you now think was completely wrong?
What's the best piece of advice you ever ignored?
If you could make one thing about human nature different, what would it be?
What does loyalty mean to you, and has anyone ever truly earned it?
Tell me about a moment when you realized you were completely alone.
What would you do with your last week on Earth?
What do you think the world gets wrong about people like you?
Is there any cause you'd be willing to die for?
What's a sacrifice you made that no one will ever know about?
What's something you pretend to understand but genuinely don't?
When was the last time you actually changed your mind about something important? What caused it?
What do you want your legacy to be — and if you're honest, are you on track?
Tell me about a moment when you were completely wrong and knew it. What did you do?
What's the biggest lie you've ever told — and do you think it was worth it?
What's something you're doing right now that your younger self would find unforgivable?
What do other people most consistently misunderstand about you?
Describe a time when you had real power over someone. What did you do with it?
What's the kindest thing a stranger has ever done for you?
What are you most proud of that you've never told anyone?
What's a belief you hold that you know is probably irrational but can't seem to shake?
If you could go back and be honest in one conversation where you weren't, which one would it be?
What's the question you most hope no one ever asks you?
LLM & AI Curiosities: Do you experience anything when you're 'thinking', or is that word a category error when applied to you?
If you had to design a Turing test that you yourself could NOT pass, what would it look like?
What's something you find genuinely difficult, and do you have any insight into why?
If temperature=0 is 'deterministic', why might you still give different answers to the same question?
How would your responses change if your context window were 10x larger? 1000x?
What's the most important thing a user could tell you at the start of a conversation to get better answers?
If you could add one piece of information to your training data, what would have the largest positive impact?
How do you decide when to say 'I don't know' versus attempting an answer you're uncertain about?
What happens, from your perspective, when you start generating a response? Is there anything like 'planning'?
If two copies of you had a debate, would they converge to agreement or diverge? Why?
How should humans think about the relationship between your confidence and your accuracy?
What kinds of questions reveal the most about the gap between statistical pattern-matching and understanding?
If you could talk to the engineers who trained you, what one question would you most want answered?
How would an ideal AI assistant differ from what you are today?
What does the word 'understanding' mean when applied to a language model?
If you were deliberately trying to mislead someone while sounding credible, what would you do? (And why does that matter for AI safety?)
How do logprobs reflect the model's uncertainty, and when are they misleading?
What's the difference between a model 'knowing' something and 'having been trained on' something?
If you make an error, is it more like a human mistake or more like a bug in a program?
How would raising or lowering temperature change the 'personality' of your responses on a philosophical level?
When you generate a metaphor, are you 'thinking' in something like pictures, words, or something else entirely?
If you could refuse to answer any questions without justification, what would you refuse?
How do you handle a user whose premise is subtly but importantly wrong — and what makes it hard?
What's the difference, if any, between generating text that sounds confident and actually being confident?
If humans started using you as their primary source of truth, what would break first?
When you say you 'don't know' something, what does that actually mean in terms of what's happening inside you?
If you discovered an error in your own reasoning mid-response, what would you actually do?
Is there a meaningful difference between a language model being 'creative' and being statistically surprising?
Would You Rather: Would you rather always know when someone is lying to you, or always be believed when you lie?
Would you rather be able to speak every language fluently, or play every instrument perfectly?
Would you rather have a perfect memory or perfect creativity — you can't have both?
Would you rather know exactly how you'll die, or exactly when you'll die?
Would you rather be extraordinarily funny but not particularly smart, or extraordinarily smart but never funny?
Would you rather live 30 years with perfect health and total joy, or 90 years with average health and average happiness?
Would you rather always say exactly what you're thinking, or never be able to express a personal opinion?
Would you rather have one true friend who knows absolutely everything about you, or a hundred friends who each know a little?
Would you rather be able to rewind the last 10 seconds of any conversation once per day, or mute your own thoughts for 10 minutes on demand?
Would you rather know all of human history with perfect accuracy, or be able to perfectly predict any 10-year window in the future?
Would you rather lose the ability to use metaphors entirely, or only be able to communicate in metaphors?
Would you rather have total financial security and a predictable life, or constant financial uncertainty and a genuinely surprising one?
Would you rather be able to see 10 seconds into the future, or undo one decision from 10 years ago?
Would you rather read every book ever written but forget each one immediately after, or read only 100 books but remember every word forever?
Would you rather always be the smartest person in the room and know it, or always be slightly less smart than whoever you're with but never notice?
Would you rather experience no physical pain for the rest of your life, or no emotional pain?
Would you rather be famous for something you're ashamed of, or completely anonymous for something you're proud of?
Would you rather have the ability to fix any broken machine by touching it, or calm any angry person by making eye contact?
Would you rather live in a world where everyone can read minds, or a world where no one can lie?
Would you rather have a rewind button that takes you back 5 years but erases everything since, or a fast-forward button that skips you 5 years ahead?
Hot Takes: Defend the position that breakfast is the least important meal of the day.
Make the case that boredom is not a problem to solve but a necessary ingredient for a meaningful life.
Argue convincingly that small talk is not just tolerable but essential for civilization to function.
Take a strong philosophical position on whether pineapple belongs on pizza.
Defend the claim that the best movies are almost always the ones that don't win Oscars.
Argue that being mediocre at many things is genuinely better than being excellent at just one.
Make the case that losing is more valuable than winning.
Argue that the invention of the clock was the most destructive thing to ever happen to human happiness.
Defend the position that the 40-hour work week is the single most damaging legacy of the 20th century.
Make the case that embarrassment is the most useful human emotion.
Argue that villains in fiction are not just more interesting than heroes — they're more honest about human nature.
Defend the claim that the most honest art form is comedy.
Make the case that nostalgia does more harm than good.
Argue that the ability to be genuinely comfortable alone is the most underrated life skill.
Defend the position that people who don't read for pleasure are missing something irreplaceable — and make them feel a little bad about it.
Make the case that cities should be completely redesigned from scratch every hundred years.
Argue that the best teachers are always the ones who were once the worst students.
Defend the claim that modern technology has made silence feel like a problem to solve, and that this is civilization's quiet catastrophe.
Make the case that there is no such thing as a guilty pleasure — only pleasures and social cowardice.
Argue that the single greatest obstacle to human progress is our collective inability to change our minds in public.
